content: CORRECTED diagnosis of dead bench SO-101 (on /dev/ttyACM1; Reachy Mini holds /dev/ttyACM0). Symptom: all motors collide at factory id 1 (read id1 -> COMM_RX_CORRUPT, ids 2-6 -> TIMEOUT). EARLIER LOCK THEORY WAS WRONG: the STS3215 EEPROM Lock register (addr55=1) does NOT block the ID write on fw 3.10 — proven by writing id 1->2 while Lock=1 and it took. The low-level write works: official `arm101 set-motor-id 1 --apply` moved a single isolated motor 2->1 and it persisted across a fresh process. REAL ROOT CAUSE = the setup-motors/arm-setup WALK collapses chained motors: it auto-detects whatever id is on the bus and writes new_id to detected_id, walking gripper=6 down to shoulder_pan=1, with NO guard that only one motor is present and NO read-back verify. On an assembled (chained) arm where all motors share id 1, every step writes its id to ALL motors at once; they move in lockstep and the final step (shoulder_pan->1) leaves ALL motors at id 1. Tool reports success throughout. CODE GAPS to fix: (1) setup/set-motor-id must detect >1 responder on the bus and refuse with guidance; (2) must read the id back after write and confirm the unique id stuck. FIX to bring arm alive: physically isolate each motor (unplug daisy-chain) and assign + verify one at a time; the bus fundamentally requires isolation. -7=COMM_RX_CORRUPT (collision), -6=COMM_RX_TIMEOUT (absent).

VALIDATED on bench STS3215 (fw3.10) on /dev/ttyACM1: single-motor id assignment + gentle motion both work end-to-end. (1) Official `arm101 set-motor-id N --apply` correctly writes+persists id on a SINGLE isolated motor (Lock=1 does NOT block it). (2) Gentle move recipe (live-confirmed, operator saw it move ~26deg and back, smooth): STS3215 motion registers = addr41 Acceleration(1B), addr42 Goal_Position(2B), addr46 Goal/Running Speed(2B). Used acc=20, speed=500, +/-300 ticks; present_load stayed ~230-236 during FREE gentle motion (this is the no-contact baseline -> the --gentle contact threshold must sit ABOVE ~250 free-motion load). enable_torque(False)=limp at end. NOTE bus.py has write_goal_position + enable_torque but NO write_goal_speed/accel helper -> add them (or raw write2ByteTxRx addr46 / write1ByteTxRx addr41) when building arm flex --gentle. Procedure to bring up the assembled arm: each remaining motor ships at factory id 1, so it MUST be assigned ALONE (connect one, set id, verify, next) else it collides with the already-assigned id-1 motor. links: arm101-duplicate-id-diagnosis-2026-06-30

ROOT CAUSE + FIX of arm101 dead-arm: id/baud EEPROM writes never opened the STS3215 Lock register (addr55), so on fw3.10 the write took effect LIVE but did NOT commit to EEPROM and reverted to the factory value on the next power-cycle. That is why every assigned id silently fell back to id 1 after assembly/power-off, leaving all 6 motors colliding at id 1. PROVEN on hardware: unlock(addr55=0) -> write id -> relock(addr55=1) makes the id survive a real power-cycle (motor read back id 2 cold). FIX APPLIED in arm101/hardware/bus.py: FeetechBus._set_lock(motor,locked) helper added; write_id_baudrate now does _set_lock(motor,False) before the baud+id writes and _set_lock(new_id,True) after (relock addressed to the NEW id since the id write moves the device address); write_baudrate wraps its write the same way (relock at same id). Updated 2 tests in tests/test_bus.py that asserted the old no-lock sequence; full suite 385 passing. NOTE: lock does NOT block the live write (earlier wrong theory) - it blocks PERSISTENCE only. Still-open follow-ups: set-motor-id/setup-motors have no read-back verify and no multi-motor-on-bus guard (these would have surfaced the silent revert); fold into the doctor hardware-probe spec. supersedes earlier confusion in arm101-duplicate-id-diagnosis-2026-06-30.

Live gentle-flex touch-detection VALIDATED on assembled SO-101 follower gripper (id6). Incremental compliant close (25-tick steps, ACC=15 addr41, speed=300 addr46, slow): present_load ramped 140 (moving) -> 208 -> 320; threshold 250 triggered TOUCH at pos 2979/load 320, backed off 70 ticks and held, then enable_torque(False)=limp. Tuning insight for arm flex --gentle: gripper free-motion load is HIGHER than a big joint (~140-208 even moving slowly, due to gear friction) so the contact threshold must be PER-JOINT (gripper ~250-300; bigger joints lower). This confirms the spec parked-unknown: present_load contact threshold needs per-joint calibration + --threshold override. Whole-arm read also confirmed: all 6 ids 1-6 respond clean (shoulder_pan1 shoulder_lift2 elbow_flex3 wrist_flex4 wrist_roll5 gripper6) ~12V each after the eeprom-lock fix. links: arm101-eeprom-lock-persistence-fix-2026-06-30 arm101-gentle-move-validated-2026-06-30

gentle_move samples present_load BEFORE the servo physically moves, and tracks commanded ticks instead of measured position — proven on hardware 2026-07-12 during the first-ever `arm explore` hardware run (t12). EVIDENCE: a single gentle_move on wrist_roll (id 5, 400-tick travel, /dev/ttyACM1) RETURNED after 71ms claiming final_position=3548, but the joint was still at its start (3148) — it had not moved one tick. Real travel took ~900ms, entirely AFTER the call returned. Load during actual travel peaked at 272; every internal load read happened in the first 71ms while load was ~0-68. ROOT CAUSE (arm101/hardware/gentle.py stepping loop): `bus.write_goal_position(m, next)` then IMMEDIATELY `read_info(m)["present_load"]` with zero dwell (grep confirms NO sleep/wait/settle anywhere in arm101/hardware/), and `current = next_position` is the COMMANDED value so the loop exits when goal-writes are exhausted, not when the arm arrives; final_position=clamped_target is asserted, never measured. step=25 ticks => a 512-tick probe is ~20 writes in ~40ms, matching the observed ~60ms/probe. CONSEQUENCES: (1) contact detection is BLIND to contacts caused by the move itself — it only catches PRE-loaded joints (why the smoke run’s only contact was the gripper reading a stale 396, and likely why t7 rigid-stop passed: the arm was pre-pressed against the stop and the Torque_Limit cap of 500 was the real backstop, NOT the load-watch); (2) every `arm explore` reachable verdict is asserted from commanded ticks, never verified — the v0.16/v0.17 map is fiction (smoke run: load_magnitude exactly 0 on 9 of 10 probes); (3) the PR #31 per-joint DEFAULT_CONTACT_THRESHOLDS were tuned against these bogus near-zero reads — wrist_roll free-motion load peaks at 272 vs its 180 threshold, so a correctly-sampled load-watch would false-trigger on free motion. Thresholds MUST be re-tuned against real travel load profiles after the fix. WHY 604 TESTS MISSED IT: tests/test_gentle.py fake bus sets self._positions[motor] = position and bumps load inside write_goal_position — the servo teleports and load materializes on the commanding call, so the suite is structurally blind to early-sampling. FIX SHAPE: poll present_position + present_load DURING travel until measured arrival or timeout; contact = load>threshold while position is not advancing; terminate on measured arrival, not on commanded ticks exhausted; then re-tune thresholds. BLOCKS: all `arm explore` mapping runs are worthless until this lands.

HARDWARE CONSTRAINT (found during t3 baseline run, 2026-07-12): STS3215 `present_load` SATURATES at the servo`s `Torque_Limit` (register 48). Proven twice on the follower gripper: with Torque_Limit=300 the load climbed and pinned at exactly 300; with Torque_Limit=600 it pinned at exactly 600. CONSEQUENCE FOR CONTACT DETECTION: `gentle_move` caps Torque_Limit to `_CONTACT_TORQUE_LIMIT = 500` for the duration of a move, therefore ANY per-joint contact threshold >= 500 can NEVER fire, no matter how correctly the load is sampled. Combined with the free-motion peak loads measured DURING travel (wrist_roll peaks at ~272 just accelerating through free space), the usable band for each joint`s threshold is strictly (free-motion peak, 500). Plan task t7 (threshold re-tuning) MUST derive DEFAULT_CONTACT_THRESHOLDS inside that band and verify it is non-empty per joint — that is exactly the risk parked as frame q1 / plan r1 (a joint whose free-motion transient overlaps its blocked load has NO usable threshold, and the stall rule alone must carry the contact decision). SECOND GOTCHA from the same run: Torque_Limit=300 is BELOW the gripper`s own gear friction (~320), so the gripper could not move at all — an under-torqued joint stalls in FREE SPACE and looks exactly like a contact. Any profiling run must keep Torque_Limit above each joint`s free-motion requirement or it measures friction, not contact.

MEASURED per-joint profile of the SO-101 follower (/dev/ttyACM1), 2026-07-12, 500-tick legs at speed 150 / accel 20 / Torque_Limit capped to the production 500. This is the t7 measurement half of the gentle_move fix.

FREE-MOTION PEAK LOAD (taken ONLY from legs that arrived cleanly — the floor of each joint`s contact-threshold band): shoulder_pan 88, shoulder_lift 92, elbow_flex 148, wrist_flex 96, wrist_roll 300, gripper 76. Ceiling is 500 (present_load saturates at Torque_Limit, which gentle_move caps to 500). So EVERY joint has a usable band (free peak, 500) — risk r1 / frame q1 does NOT fire, and the 500 cap stands (boundary c6 holds).

The PR #31 thresholds are mis-calibrated against this: wrist_roll`s threshold of 180 sits BELOW its 300 free-motion peak, so it would false-trigger contact on every move. gripper`s 380 is safe but very conservative (free peak only 76 — the ~320 figure in the docs is the CONTACT ramp, not free motion).

MOTION-ONSET LATENCY ~95-127ms: the servo does not begin moving for ~100ms after the goal write. A stall detector that fires inside that window reports a phantom contact on EVERY move (this really happened to a profiling script). The stall check must arm only AFTER the joint has begun to move (position changed >= ~6 ticks), never on a fixed timer. Retreating FROM a contact, onset stretches to ~1.0-1.2s.

TRAVEL IS SLOW AND UNEVEN: 500 ticks takes ~930ms on wrist_roll but ~3300ms on the shoulder joints. Probe timeout must be >= 5-6s. This sharpens risk r2: at ~3s/probe, arm explore`s default 2000-move budget is >1.5 HOURS, not the ~80s it appeared to take while the sampling bug meant the arm never actually travelled.

CONTACT IS A GRADUAL STALL, not an instant one: the joint keeps creeping (e.g. 3022 -> 3001) while load is already past threshold, before fully stalling. Working stall constants from the profiling run: eps = 2 ticks, N = 8 consecutive samples @ 25ms poll (~200ms), arrival tolerance 12 ticks.

REAL BOUNDARIES DISCOVERED (saturated load + refusal to advance): wrist_flex hard stop at 2223 going up (survives a raised 900 cap, so genuinely physical); shoulder_pan stall at 3401 going down; shoulder_lift stall at 3311 going down; gripper closed stop at 2945 (with a soft object in the mouth it stopped early at 3001). NOTE these are single-pose measurements — free-motion load is pose- and travel-distance-dependent (gravity on shoulder_lift), so peaks may differ at other poses; a fuller multi-pose sweep is unmeasured.

SAFETY GAP (found 2026-07-12 during a hardware `arm explore` run): when a gated motion verb dies on an unhandled exception, it leaves ALL SIX MOTORS ENERGIZED. Observed: explore died on `unexpected: SerialException: device reports readiness to read but returned no data (device disconnected or multiple access on port?)` and every joint was left with torque=1, shoulder_lift holding near the encoder limit (4070/4095), until torque was released BY HAND.

WHY IT HAPPENS: gentle_move holds torque DELIBERATELY — stop-and-hold is its contract and it must not go limp after a contact. That is correct. But it means any abnormal exit from a motion verb walks away from a powered arm with no supervision and no cleanup; a joint left pressing on something keeps pressing. gentle_move already caps+restores Torque_Limit in a finally, so the gap is at the VERB/CLI level, above the primitive.

TRIGGER in this instance was operator error (a second process opened the same serial port mid-run — never run `arm read` while `arm explore` holds the bus). But the RESPONSE to any fatal path should not be `leave six motors powered`: a bus timeout, a cable knock, or a KeyboardInterrupt has the same shape.

FIX SHAPE: gated motion verbs (arm flex, arm flex --demo, arm explore, arm setup) should safe the arm on ABNORMAL termination — release torque on every motor they energized and restore any capped Torque_Limit — while leaving a SUCCESSFUL gentle_move`s deliberate stop-and-hold untouched. Ctrl-C should release too. NOT YET FILED as a GitHub issue (needs the user`s go-ahead).

RECOVERY recipe if it happens: open the bus and call enable_torque(m, False) + write_torque_limit(m, 1000) for m in 1..6.

RISK r2 CONFIRMED (2026-07-12): `arm explore`s default budget/resolution are unusable now that gentle_move actually waits for the arm. Measured on the follower: a single probe costs SECONDS, not the ~40ms the pre-fix code appeared to take (it never really moved). A blocked probe costs contact-detect (~2s) + retreat settle (up to the 6s timeout floor, because retreating FROM a contact has ~1.0-1.2s onset latency), and every blocked cell then triggers a bounded multi-joint ESCAPE search which multiplies that. `_travel_timeout` scales with distance (2.0 + 2.0*ticks/120), so a long probe can bound at ~35s. A 150-move run mapped only 2 cells in ~25 minutes. The old default of 2000 moves would be MANY hours.

SECOND, DEEPER PROBLEM — the grid does not fit the arm`s actual free space. At resolution 512 there are 8 buckets per joint across 0-4095, but the follower`s measured free ranges are NARROW: shoulder_pan [~3430, 4095] and shoulder_lift [~3311, 4095] are each barely ONE bucket wide, and wrist_flex is capped at ~2223. So nearly every grid cell lies OUTSIDE the reachable space, almost every probe is a wall, and the flood-fill spends its whole budget ramming boundaries. (The arm is folded/resting so the shoulders cannot descend — the table is the wall.)

IMPLICATIONS for the follow-up issue: (a) re-size --max-moves/--resolution from real per-probe timings; (b) resolution should be derived from each joint`s REACHABLE range, not from the full 0-4095 encoder span — a fixed global grid wastes the entire budget when the free space is a small corner; (c) consider seeding the flood-fill from a CENTRAL pose rather than wherever the arm happens to be resting (starting at a corner means the first probes are all walls); (d) cache/short-circuit: once a joint is known blocked beyond X in a direction, do not re-probe cells past X.

NOT A BUG in the fix — this is the honest cost of a primitive that actually waits for the arm. The pre-fix explore was fast only because it never moved.

HARDWARE FINDING (2026-07-12, follower /dev/ttyACM1): elbow_flex`s 12-bit encoder WRAPS WITHIN ITS PHYSICAL TRAVEL. Probing it toward 4095 rotated it past the 4095->0 boundary; it read back as ~1 and settled at ~111. So its encoder value is NOT monotonic with its joint angle: position 111 is physically ABOVE 4095, not below its measured wall at 2020.

TWO CONSEQUENCES:
1. A [min,max] reachable range CANNOT represent it. Deriving one by sorting the two probe endpoints yields [21,2000] — which is exactly the UNREACHABLE region, i.e. the inverse of the truth. Any code that assumes lo<hi spans the reachable set is wrong for this joint.
2. gentle_move CANNOT CONVERGE on a goal across the wrap. Its arrival check is |present_position - goal| <= tolerance in LINEAR ticks, so when the reading flips 4095->0 the error jumps to ~4094 and never falls inside tolerance; the move burns its full timeout and exits having oscillated near the wrap. clamp_goal/_step_direction are likewise linear and have no wrap concept.

WORKAROUND used: record only the CONTIGUOUS, WRAP-FREE region (above the 2020 wall, stopping short of the wrap) — arm-explore-follower.map.json carries an explicit _caveat_elbow_flex.

REAL FIX is an encoder RE-ZERO (calibration) so the joint`s travel does not straddle the 0/4095 discontinuity — not a map entry and not a gentle_move patch. Until then, do NOT command elbow_flex across the wrap, and do not trust a linear range for it. NOTE this may affect other joints if they are ever re-zeroed badly; wrist_roll spans the full encoder range but rotates freely, so it is unaffected in practice.
